speaker: interviewer
text: Standby. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape three. Interview with Dr. Paul Parks, October 23, 1995. How long were you in-- no, no-- actually, where were you in basic training? What  camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, what happened was the system was this way. At some point, we went through-- at Fort Benjamin Harrison-- we took all those basic examinations. We took the examinations-- the academic kinds, and the psychological examinations. All those occurred at Fort Benjamin Harrison. When they got through with those, now in retrospect I know that they would-- they use those examinations and our background and experiences, whatever we were, to make a judgment about what-- what branch of the service they would send you to. And also to what camp they would send you to.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened was they talked about-- to me about being a draftsman, 'cause I was in engineering school, and I had taken drafting and things of the sort. And they-- finally, one day they called us all out and they had trains all over the place and they told us to pack our bags. And we came out, and at that point, they gave each one of us a-- a number. And the number related to a train, and when they got through, all of us was on a train going somewhere. And we happened to be going to-- the group I was with was all going to Camp Lee, Virginia, which was down outside of Richmond in Petersburg, Virginia.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now they put us on trains. And what happened was the train-- they told us later-- I was talking to one of the people who ran-- one of the conductors. He said they were taking us over a lot of unused tracks so they wouldn't bog down the commercial traffic.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went all the way down from-- from Fort Benjamin Harrison across Cincinnati. And in Cincinnati the guy told us, now, they're, all loaded, the black soldiers, up in the front cars.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I always wondered what that was about. When we got to Cincinnati, he told us this is the reason why we did it. Because we're in the South now, and you have to-- you have to ride on the first car. All black soldiers ride on the first cars, and the white soldiers ride on the back cars. And you finally learned why-- because they weren't air conditioned. You know, it was summertime. [INAUDIBLE] you open the windows, you're getting cinders. The black soldiers were getting cinders. You know, our uniforms getting all dirty.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what they told us to do, at some point, they told us to pull the shades on the-- on our-- on the windows. And we wondered, why? What's this about? And when I looked-- I peeped behind the shade, knowing me-- I peeped outside the shade and there was these guys standing on the [? side ?] with these rifles and shotguns who were standing on the platform as we went by. And I asked the conductor, what's that about? He said, well, you know, we've had several black soldiers shot on these trains. Because they shoot at black soldiers when they see them. That's why they had us pull the shades, so they wouldn't know that you were black soldiers in these cars. Who were the people shooting?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, white folk.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, just ordinary people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ordinary people. They were just-- you know, they would come out the hills of Kentucky, I guess. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the fact that you were a college student determine where you were sent and what branch? What branch were you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was sent to the quartermaster corps at that time. I think it did. I think that they made a judgment that I would go to the quartermaster corps because of my educational background. And they wanted to train me to do-- to be-- to be a draftsman, they said. When I got to Camp Lee, however, Camp Lee did not have black soldiers becoming draftsmen. So Camp Lee decided they would train me to be a motor machinist because I had-- somewhere in the end of my college there was a program that Roosevelt had then, the National Youth Administration, they called it NYA. They-- I got into-- my mother got me into the NYA, and I learned to be a machinist. That was what they trained. As a kid, I learned to-- you know, but-- between the time I went-- finished high school and went to college, that summer, they were training me to be a machinist. So I had mentioned that in-- in my-- in my-- when they was questioning about my background. So they sent me up to be a motor machinist. And they were training me to be that after my basic training.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in the infantry?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or the--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was quartermaster corps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Quartermaster corps.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So what happened was when I got to-- when I got to Camp Lee, the-- the 11th and 12th regiment was of black soldiers. They were all the way-- these lined up from one to 11 and 12 were the-- were the areas and each one of the regiments.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were at the tail end of all the regiments. We were the last thing before you got into the woods, was the black regiments.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now in Camp Lee, our officers were white. The non-commissioned officers were black. And that's where we started our training. We started our training. And we-- the drinking fountains were white and black. The restroom in our area were all black, but if you went down to the service center, you had to go into the black restroom as opposed to the white restroom. They were labeled on the door at the service center.

speaker: interviewer
text: So every time I went down to the service center-- they had dances and all for us-- but they still, even in a black-- black-- in the black area, they had white and black restrooms. And they had white and black drinking fountains. And it was always the fact the black drinking fountains were dirty. So I drank out of the white drinking fountains when I needed to. So I didn't realize that they were keeping-- somebody was making a record of my violations, as they called them. And I-- you know, this is me. So they were supposed to-- we were supposed to get on the back of the bus. The way they-- the way they did that was to come up and start loading up first in our-- our regiments, which were at the tail end. So they'd tell you to move to the rear, because you got on, you've got to move to the rear. And you can only fill up a certain percentage of the bus, and then they moved the buses down to the white regiments and they always end up in the front. So I used to walk down two regiments and get on with the white soldiers. [LAUGHS] And so somebody evidently reported that also. 'Cause later on, I'll tell you, they-- they-- they knew about it. So then the first part of your basic training was learning how to do all those things like marching and firing weapons, and all-- all-- all the Army stuff was in the first part. That was about-- it was a 13-week program-- so the first six weeks, or the first seven weeks, really, was all the Army things. About-- they teach you about-- they taught us about gas and all the other things, the weapons. And they-- and they had all those propaganda movies about kill or be killed, and all the other things they talked about.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- then venereal disease. They had a big thing about that, and they showed us these horrible pictures about venereal diseases and things of the sort. But all of that was your basic training.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the propaganda films indicate some of the things that were going on in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. The propaganda things were all about the enemy. The enemy was Germany, that we were seeing. And they talked about how it was kill or be killed. They talked about how bad the Germans were. Didn't talk about anything at all about the Germans and Jews. And as I look back on it, it's very interesting because they talked about how they'd come into the lowlands and they'd destroyed people. They'd taken people-- like France-- and they took all these countries. And then they showed us a picture of how England was standing off alone and fighting off the Germans, and how horrible they were. And every film ended with, you must learn to kill or be killed. So they were training us to be-- I always said training us to be low paid hired assassins. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- so we learned, and they taught us about everything we did. When they showed these targets we were shooting at, many times they were Germans. And they pop up, and you shoot at them. So everything was geared to getting us mentally and physically involved with killing Germans. How long was basic training?

speaker: interviewee
text: 13 Weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven weeks was this sort of thing. They never stopped showing us movies. But the-- the last six weeks was in the special training part. That's where I went up and learned to be a motor machinist in those six weeks. But in the evenings or afternoons when you came back from-- marched back from your training, you got some more pictures about what-- the Germans. And-- Now I didn't have to go very far, because my father was a disabled veteran. And as far as I was concerned, that had happened because of Germans again. And my father had talked about the Germans, and I had a different picture. I'd already had this picture of Germans being bad folk from my father. And he talked about how cruel they were and things that they did. And here I am seeing it again in the movies. So all this became very real to me. There was-- none of that was propaganda. It was all just fact, as far as I was concerned. Because it was underscoring what my father'd already told me. As my father said, he wished he could change places for me and go with me and go take my place in the war rather than me having to go. So I was all sensitized to it. And so-- but when we went up to-- went up to-- up the river-- up the road to take our special training, they had these white and black drinking fountains again. And they were still the dirty black ones and the clean white ones. And I-- I started drinking out of-- I'm not gonna drink out of that dirty fountain.

speaker: interviewer
text: Guy said, you're gonna get in trouble. I said, hey, what can they do?' So I think-- and what happened was at some point, we finished-- we finished that training. And then once you finish all your 13 weeks, you have to sit around waiting on your assignment.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had applied for ASTP, which was a college program to go back to school, which they were sending a lot of kids back. I'd also applied for OCS-- officers training. They all told me I was a little bit young for-- for officer training, but they would see what they could do. I had scored very well in all my issues and they said they were gonna try to get me in there with my non-commissioned officers. What happened was I went to Richmond on a three-day pass. Coming back at the end of the three-day pass, I stepped back to let a black woman on in front of me who had groceries, and I told her to get on the bus in front of me. And this white soldier screamed, don't let that black bitch on in front of you! And I hit him. And we started-- a mini riot occurred. The guys turned over the bus and all the black soldiers took the square in Richmond for about three or four hours until the MPs came with [INAUDIBLE] 50 caliber machine guns and told us to get on the buses and go back to camp. And going back, they said, we're not gonna tell anybody what happened here.' And I said, fine. They quarantined us. They put MPs around our barracks, and we couldn't come out except under escort. And at one point after about three or four days of that, I was ordered to come-- to put on my dress uniform and come with these two MPs to headquarters-- to the headquarters. And I went to the headquarters office and when I got there, there was a fellow. They ushered me into this room. It was a black lieutenant colonel, Colonel Snowden, who's from Boston. He's lived in Boston, his family. The Snowdens are the people who own Freedom-- who ran Freedom House in Boston. That was-- that was Otto Snowden's father.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I went in, he was the adjutant. He was the deputy commander of the post. And I guess they had him there for the black soldiers. And he said to me, I understand you caused trouble in Richmond. And I said-- he'll understand what I'm talking about. So I explained it to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, look, son, you gotta learn to live in the South. Now you should not have a-- you should not have started this problem. You should have just mind your business and not gotten into trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, how could I stand there and have this happen? And he said, you're in the South and you're gonna have to learn to do that. He said, now, I-- I'm gonna put you on extra duty. And what I want you to do is go back, and we're gonna issue you a pair of gloves, some white gloves. And I want you to come and to-- to be the doormen and open and close the door of the white officers as they go into their officer's club. And I said, I'll go to hell first. And he said, you may well. Go back to your barracks. You're saying you're going to disobey the orders of a field-grade officer? And I said, I guess that's what I'm doing, sir.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, Sooner or later, I knew I'd come to the end of this whole sequence. And I guess this is it. Because I'm not gonna do that. He said, you go back to your own barracks and you'll hear from me later.' And these MPs took me back. And what they did, about three days later, the MPs came and said, pack your things and get your duffel bags. And they had an order that I was to go to Camp Shanks, New York as a casualty replacement. That I had been-- I had been denied-- I had been denied going to college. I'd been denied going to OCS. I'd been denied all of that. And I-- and they took me under guard to Camp Shanks, New York. And I remember again, I was dressed in my dress uniform. They had me go into this place and change my dress uniform and put on fatigues and-- and pack-- and repack my duffel bags and come back and get in line with these guys. And I got in line, not knowing who they were. I ain't know what outfit I was in. I just knew that I was going to get on the-- and the was the boat that we were going on. And it was a single ship that ran the-- didn't have a convoy. It was running by itself. And they said because it was such a fast ship, that we didn't have to have an escort. So I got--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In fact, I saw a picture of the in a little store up on Boylston Street the other day. It was a part of the-- and now I know it was-- it was one of the Cunard line ships.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when I got on board, I asked this fellow, this white officer, what outfit am I in? He said, you're in the combat engineers. I said, I don't know anything about combat engineers! I was training to be a quartermaster. He said, you're gonna learn now.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he trained me how to use the-- how to disassemble and assemble the M1s and things [? like so ?] while we were on the boat. But I knew that if I lost sight of him, I don't know how would I ever get off that boat. 'Cause I didn't know a soul. And I followed him around wherever he went.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally, one day I was on, I guess about the third day out. 'Cause I never had a problem with seasickness. So the first day out, I-- nobody came down 'cause everybody was seasick. So I gathered people's oranges and all, and I had a ball.

speaker: interviewee
text: Second day out, I saw this man bending over the rails. And I said, that person looks familiar. And lo and behold, it was my father's youngest brother, my uncle. And he was on his way to Europe, and he was a part of an aircraft-- in the air corps. He wasn't flying. He was the person who was one of the mechanics.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, where are you staying? I said, I'm down in the hole. He backs down, and he said, 'I don't want my nephew down there. You come up and you stay with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said he had a-- he had a room in which there were two beds, like a stateroom. And-- 'cause the Air Force always got the best accommodations. So I stayed with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, 'cause if somebody hits the boat or anything, you're down below. How can you get out? So I stayed with my uncle. Were the troops on that boat all black?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was a big ship. There were blacks, whites, everybody was on board.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you headed to?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were headed to the Firth of Clyde. We ended up in the Firth of Clyde in Scotland, outside of Glasgow. And at some point, we ended up in-- in Scotland. My-- I went down and got with my guys, with the guys I was with, with my lieutenant again. And he said, we're getting off. Lieutenant Swig was his name. It just came to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, we're getting off. And we unloaded in Scotland. And that was the first time I had heard about men in kilts. It was the first time I ever saw a guy in a kilt. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: One of my fellows said, hey, they're wearing dresses over here. [LAUGHS] 'Cause none of us knew [INAUDIBLE]. It was foreign to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: I had read about it, so I knew. I'd read about the Black Watch, you know, that sort of thing, in books I got from the library. So I was ahead of a lot of the rest of the guys. But they were really-- one guy kept asking the guy, what are you wearing under the-- under the kilt? [LAUGHS] And he wouldn't-- he wouldn't tell him.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, we had-- everybody was very friendly. So they put us on trains, and they took us down-- they broke up our outfit into companies. My company-- there were two companies of us who went to Yeovil in Somerset. And we were in a camp-- Houndstone Barracks, I think it was, in-- in Yeovil. The thing that amazed me was we went-- we got there in the middle of the night. And we were awakened that next morning by British youngsters, boys and girls running all over the camp waking us up, saying hello to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was saying, how'd these people get into a military camp? But they were, and the people were just-- they were outrageous. They were just very, very nice to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was the first time that I can remember in my life, outside of Daniel, that I had a-- just-- just that kind of relationship with white youngsters. It was just a different world. It was very foreign to me. I didn't know what to suspect-- expect. And I thought, you know, be careful 'cause you still don't know what these people are gonna do to you. I don't know what that was. But see, you have to know that when I was in high school, every day, every week, we had auditorium. And they would bring in somebody who was well-renowned in the black-- an artist or a painter or a philosopher or a professor, and they would talk to us. But everybody had the same theme. If you're gonna make it in this country, you gotta be better than white folk doing it. OK. You gotta exceed what white folks can do. And that-- Purdue caused me a lot of trouble and that caused me a lot of trouble at Purdue. Because I couldn't be better than all those kids. And now I'm dealing-- you know, it's one thing to say that when you're in high school, and you're not competing with anybody white. But when you get to Purdue and you're sitting there in this all white environment, now to be better than all those folk gets to be a very difficult one. And if you don't, if you aren't better, you feel like that that's failure. And you're gonna drop off the planet somewhere. Because you just won't make it. I remember when I went into a chemistry class. When I was in Purdue, the first class I went into was a huge chemistry class, laboratory. And this guy, Dr. Miller, who was head of the chemistry department, told me-- and my mother said, never sit in the back of a room. Sit down front. I got there, there was only seats were vacant when I finally found this crazy place were seats in the back. So I'm sitting in the back as the only black person in this sea of about 200 white kids. And-- and all males. They were engineers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he asked-- and he called my name and said, come down front. So I went down wondering, what is this about? I went down, and he told me to open my hand, and he put a-- put the chemical blue vitriol in my hand and put water on it, and said, 'Go in the corner. When it starts to sting you, you dance a little jig for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I held my hand like this, and I went in the corner. Now you have to understand, now he's dealing with the discipline of an [? Indian. ?] I would stand there while it burned through my head before I'd even say anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm standing there and he finally said, that thing hasn't burned yet? And I looked at him and he says, come over here and wash your hand off. He said, you're a smart aleck. He said, I don't want you in my class. I said, what I'm supposed to take? He said, you just find someplace else to go. I don't want you in here. So I remember going to my math class the same one. And the guy stood in the door and said, I don't teach niggers. Go somewhere-- you get another class.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was that sort-- that was my experience. And here I am in a world that's entirely different. Where people are actually happy to see me and all of us. And so we stayed in-- we stayed in-- in Yeovil for about a month. And from Yeovil, they sent us to Wadebridge, which is in Cornwall. And Wadebridge was where I really, really had-- in spite of the Army and in spite of all the things-- was one of the best experiences I had in the Army. I went-- when we got-- got to Wadebridge, I was standing on guard one night. And this fellow came up in a collar. He was a minister. And he identified himself as the bishop of Cornwall-- the Methodist bishop of Cornwall-- a Reverend Matthew Harvey.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said-- we talked for a long time, and he said, son, why don't you come by my house when you get off? You can-- in-- when-- the evenings when you get a chance to leave here, why don't you to come by? And he says, it's called Cavendish House. And he told me the directions to get to Cavendish House.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went there and met his wife. And it was-- this was an amazing place for me. It was beautiful. And, you know, I saw-- this house was just extraordinary and it had Cavendish House all up on the door. And it was just something I'd read about and just couldn't believe that I'd ever see and be involved in. And I went to see him, and he had this gorgeous library. And he said, the library's there. Anything you want to read, you read. And he took me in. And he would send letters to my mother. And she would write him, and he'd tell her how I was getting along. And when he would go on his trips out to the-- like, he would go-- he went down to-- to Tintagel, the place where King Arthur's court was supposed to be, and down to the [INAUDIBLE], and he would go, you know, through his parish. And he would take me with him many times, whenever I could get up, you know, leave. He would take me through to-- through the countryside into the places where-- in his parish.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's see. I got there in '40-- the first-- '43. It must have been-- must've been somewhere around the summer of '43. I would suspect it was somewhere around June or July, as I remember. And I stayed in England until the invasion, till we went to the invasion of Normandy--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is June 6th--

speaker: interviewee
text: --which is June 6th. So we left--

speaker: interviewee
text: --'44. But we stayed in Wadebridge longer than we stayed anywhere else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. At that point-- at what point did you become aware-- or did you ever become aware of Nazism and extermination camps and concentration camps and stuff like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: None of that to me now.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing I knew that-- in Wadebridge, I went to Plymouth a couple of times. The Army sent me to Plymouth a couple of times. And we saw the Germans. We could not go-- and they wouldn't allow American soldiers to go into the air raid shelters.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I stood on the street and watched them strafing the streets, the Germans. Always thought they were awful people because what would happen was, they would always strafe the street the time the children were coming home from school. And I thought what a horrible thing, you know, on the daylight raids.

speaker: interviewee
text: But every night, they'd come over and they would-- they would bomb London. And those days, it was-- it was planes bombing London. It was before the V-2s and V-1s.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were coming over bombing and the aircraft-- anti-aircraft guns were firing at them. And you see the searchlights looking for them and trying to bring them down. And then those amazing guys in the Spitfires. 'Cause when we saw them in-- at-- when we were in Somerset, we were right next to the RAF airfields. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And those-- damn. You talk about courage? Those guys would take four planes and go up and attack a whole German group coming over. And they were amazing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about the invasion itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK--

speaker: interviewer
text: By the way, when you went, were you still part of a black unit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we were still the 365th. That's what I joined the 365th in-- in-- in New York at Camp Shanks.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Camp Shanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was still with them. And I had been-- I had been progressing. At the time of the invasion, I had moved from a private to a-- to a tech sergeant, because I was a platoon sergeant. And what happened was, we were in-- first we were in Leicester up in the Midlands. And it was in Leicester that we ran into the 82nd Airborne. And there were-- there were literal race riots in Leicester. We got into fights with the 82nd. What happened was one day, I was-- we were-- we had been invited to a dance, several of us. And went to the dance and this black soldier was coming down the steps with this young English lady, his date. When he got near the front door, we heard the machine gun go off and both of them were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: By whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the 82nd Airborne. They were riding by with their guns on-- mounted on the jeeps and they strafed the front of the building. And from that point on-- and we had been-- we had been searched before. So we couldn't leave the camp with any-- any weapons because we were searched very, very vigorously to be sure we weren't carrying anything, any weapons at all. And here these guys were shooting at our folk. And then we got-- several of them tried to stop us on the street and we got into a street fight with them. It was just awful. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: So Eisenhower finally declared the town off limits because of these fights that we were having with the-- with the 82nd. That was-- that General Gavin was there, and it was-- they were at the same camp. We were both occupying the same camp. It was a huge camp up in Leicester.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so Eisenhower declared the town off limits. And he said that the black soldiers-- and then he said-- what he changed-- he said-- he'd allow the black soldiers to go downtown to-- to the places where the bars and all were. But the white soldiers would have to go to-- they could go to the suburbs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, we're going to change our tape.
